impacts.caseName: Teva Pharmaceuticals International GmbH v. Eli Lilly and Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in granting judgment as a matter of law that Teva's anti-CGRP antibody headache treatment patents were invalid for failure to satisfy the written-description and enablement requirements of 35 U.S.C. § 112.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's JMOL of invalidity for Teva's headache patents covering humanized anti-CGRP antagonist antibodies used to treat headache. The jury had found willful infringement and no invalidity, and the Federal Circuit reinstated those findings. This is a major pharmaceutical patent ruling affecting the migraine treatment market involving Teva's Ajovy product and Lilly's competing product.
impacts.positiveImplications: Strengthens patent protection for biopharmaceutical innovators by confirming that patents claiming a genus of humanized antibodies can satisfy written description and enablement requirements even when only one specific antibody is disclosed, provided the specification references known prior-art methods.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors like Eli Lilly face renewed infringement liability for competing anti-CGRP treatments, potentially limiting market entry and competition in the lucrative migraine therapy space.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of the patent holder's unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the unpatented products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the jury's finding that Ingevity engaged in unlawful tying by conditioning licenses to its fuel-vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court found sufficient evidence that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems not covered by the patent), making Ingevity's conduct anticompetitive rather than protected by patent law.
impacts.positiveImplications: Protects competitors like BASF from exclusionary patent-leveraging practices, ensuring that unpatented products with substantial non-infringing uses remain open to competition.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who bundle patent licenses with exclusive purchasing requirements for related unpatented goods face significant antitrust liability, limiting their ability to leverage patent portfolios into adjacent product markets.

impacts.caseName: The Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods for detecting anomalous program executions using emulators and models of function calls are directed to abstract ideas under 35 U.S.C. § 101, and whether damages for foreign sales of infringing software are recoverable.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted cybersecurity patents were abstract at Alice step one and remanding for further proceedings on step two. The court also ruled that damages based on Norton's foreign sales were improperly awarded. The decision also vacated enhanced damages and attorneys' fees tied to an improper contempt finding against Norton's counsel.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies benefit from strengthened patent eligibility scrutiny for software patents directed to abstract concepts, reducing exposure to large infringement verdicts. The foreign-sales ruling limits extraterritorial patent damages.
impacts.negativeImplications: Universities and patent holders with cybersecurity and software-method patents face heightened invalidity risk under § 101, potentially reducing the value of significant patent portfolios in the software space.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming server-side methods for tailoring content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's streaming content patents were ineligible under § 101, reversing the jury's infringement finding and $2.5 million damages award in favor of Netflix. The court found that the claims were directed to the abstract idea of tailoring content to device capabilities, a concept long practiced in the art. This required entry of judgment for Netflix.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and tech companies like Netflix gain significant protection from patent assertions based on abstract content-delivery concepts, reducing litigation risk from patent assertion entities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the adaptive streaming and content delivery space face increased difficulty in enforcing patents that claim server-side content optimization methods.

impacts.caseName: VLSI Technology LLC v. Intel Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether multicore processor patent claims requiring core selection at 'a given voltage' or 'a specified voltage' are infringed when the accused products perform the selection process at a design stage outside the United States, and whether doctrine of equivalents can overcome a literal noninfringement finding.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit partially reversed summary judgment of noninfringement for Intel, finding that genuine disputes of material fact existed regarding VLSI's doctrine of equivalents theory. However, the court affirmed that VLSI's damages expert theories were properly struck for inadequate disclosure. The decision addresses important questions about extraterritorial application of patent law to semiconductor design processes.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders asserting semiconductor and processor patents retain viable infringement theories under the doctrine of equivalents even when literal infringement arguments face extraterritoriality challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Semiconductor companies face continued exposure to patent litigation over multicore processor design choices, with extraterritoriality defenses not dispositive at the summary judgment stage.

impacts.caseName: International Medical Devices, Inc. v. Cornell
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the plaintiff met its burden under California law to demonstrate that each of four asserted trade secrets constituted protectable trade secrets, and whether ideas that are generally known in the field can support an inventive contribution for patent inventorship purposes.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the trade secret verdict, finding insufficient evidence that the asserted trade secrets were protectable because the underlying information was generally known. The court also reversed the patent inventorship correction, holding that generally known ideas cannot constitute inventive contributions. However, the court affirmed the counterfeiting verdict for unauthorized use of the Penuma® trademark.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants in trade secret cases benefit from heightened scrutiny of whether asserted secrets are truly protectable, particularly when the underlying information is generally known in the field.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trade secret plaintiffs face greater burden in establishing protectability, and businesses that rely on trade secret protection for information that may be considered generally known in their industry risk losing legal protection entirely.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on both inequitable conduct in obtaining patents and Walker Process fraud claims for attempted monopolization in the coiled tubing industry.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment in both directions, finding triable issues on whether Tenaris committed inequitable conduct by failing to disclose prior CYMAX documents during patent prosecution, and on whether Global Tubing could prove Walker Process fraud for attempted monopolization. The case involves patents central to the oil and gas coiled tubing market.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors challenging patents obtained through potentially inequitable conduct retain the ability to pursue both unenforceability and antitrust remedies when material fact disputes exist.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the oil and gas industry face renewed litigation risk on both inequitable conduct and antitrust claims, with potential exposure to treble damages under Walker Process if fraud is proven at trial.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a Rule 30(b)(6) corporate representative may testify at summary judgment about facts outside their personal knowledge, and whether computer source code constitutes hearsay for purposes of proving prior art under the on-sale bar of 35 U.S.C. § 102(b).
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment of invalidity, holding that Rule 30(b)(6) testimony is admissible at summary judgment even when the witness lacks personal knowledge, and that computer source code describing computer commands is not hearsay. The case invalidated engine-controller reprogramming patents based on prior commercial sales of the Hypertech Power Programmer III.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants in patent cases gain powerful evidentiary tools, as 30(b)(6) corporate representative testimony about historical sales is admissible regardless of personal knowledge, and source code is directly usable as non-hearsay evidence of prior art.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face increased vulnerability to prior-art challenges based on historical product sales, even when direct witnesses are unavailable, as corporate designees and source code may now more easily prove the on-sale bar.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission correctly found that Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement features infringed Masimo's patents on wearable pulse oximetry devices, and whether Masimo satisfied the domestic industry requirement.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated Section 337 by importing Apple Watch models infringing Masimo's wearable blood oxygen measurement patents. The court upheld both the validity and infringement determinations as well as the limited exclusion order barring importation of infringing watches. This is a major ruling in the ongoing Apple-Masimo patent dispute over wearable health technology.
impacts.positiveImplications: Medical technology innovators like Masimo are strongly protected by ITC exclusion orders that can block major consumer electronics imports, vindicating investment in patented health-sensing technology.
impacts.negativeImplications: Consumer electronics companies incorporating health-monitoring features face significant import restrictions and must design around validated medical device patents or secure licenses.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of a refiled district court declaratory judgment action when it had previously missed the statutory deadline for seeking a stay of the original action involving the same parties and issues.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not obtain a mandatory stay of Ascendis's refiled declaratory judgment action by dismissing and refiling its own action after missing the 30-day deadline in the original case. The court found this an impermissible end-run around the statutory time limits of § 1659(a). This ruling ensures pharmaceutical declaratory judgment actions proceed in parallel with ITC investigations.
impacts.positiveImplications: Pharmaceutical companies seeking declaratory judgment of non-infringement benefit from the inability of ITC complainants to manipulate mandatory-stay provisions through strategic refiling of actions.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC complainants lose the ability to delay parallel district court proceedings through tactical refiling after missing statutory stay deadlines, potentially requiring them to litigate on two fronts simultaneously.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Patent Trial and Appeal Board correctly construed the term 'encryption information' in claims directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether the Board's construction improperly allowed it to sustain the patent over Netflix's obviousness challenge.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction in DivX's streaming encryption patent and vacated the Board's decision rejecting Netflix's obviousness challenge. The court adopted a broader construction of 'encryption information' and remanded for the Board to reconsider obviousness under the corrected construction. This affects patents governing digital rights management in streaming media.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming platforms benefit from the potential invalidation of DRM-related patents that could otherwise restrict streaming technology implementation.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the DRM and encrypted streaming space face renewed invalidity challenges when claim terms are broadly construed on appeal.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent damages expert report and granting summary judgment of no remedy based on the absence of admissible damages evidence.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report on its network optimization patents asserted against Microsoft's Azure platform, finding the district court applied an overly rigid standard. The court vacated summary judgment that had been granted solely because no damages evidence remained after the exclusion. This ruling provides important guidance on the admissibility of patent damages evidence in cloud computing disputes.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from a more permissive standard for damages expert testimony, reducing the risk that Daubert challenges will effectively eliminate their case before trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Large technology companies like Microsoft face renewed patent infringement exposure in cloud infrastructure disputes when damages experts are permitted to testify on broader methodologies.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations as confidential is consistent with the governing statute and the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's holding that the ITC's blanket confidentiality practice for questionnaire responses is incompatible with 19 U.S.C. §§ 1516a and 1677f, and that the statute does not abrogate the common law right of public access to judicial records. The decision requires the ITC to make individualized confidentiality determinations rather than automatically designating all submissions as confidential.
impacts.positiveImplications: Domestic industries and trade practitioners gain greater transparency in antidumping and countervailing duty proceedings, with increased access to information that may have been improperly shielded from public view.
impacts.negativeImplications: Foreign producers and importers who submit business information in trade investigations face reduced confidentiality protections and must affirmatively justify confidential treatment, increasing the risk of competitive information disclosure.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Patent Trial and Appeal Board governing discretionary denials of inter partes review petitions constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions guiding Board decisions on discretionary IPR non-institution are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, the Director retains ultimate authority, and the instructions do not bind the Director. This preserves the PTO's flexibility in managing its IPR docket.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's retained flexibility to decline IPR institution, reducing the risk of duplicative patent challenges and preserving patent stability.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies frequently targeted by patent assertions lose their challenge to the PTO's discretionary denial framework, meaning more IPR petitions may be denied without formal rulemaking constraints on the process.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund projects unrelated to operating the PACER system.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million nationwide class settlement resolving claims that the federal judiciary charged excessive PACER fees in violation of the E-Government Act. The court upheld the settlement terms, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to class representatives, finding that hundreds of thousands of PACER users would be fully or substantially reimbursed. The decision validates the legal theory that PACER fees may only fund PACER operations.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses, law firms, and organizations that heavily use PACER will receive reimbursement for overcharges and can expect more constrained fee practices going forward.
